But it also defies scripting in the same ways that many of the other behavioral therapies are amendable to (In week 1 ... In week 2 ...). 

Ultimately, I wonder if its just it's own thing. While I haven't figured out how to succiently capture the spirit of that thing I'll keep trying/looking. /3

posts.text: I'm trying to pinpoint the equivalents of contemporary psychodynamic psychotherapy. Some of medicine has the "see class X do things Y" flavor to it (where in many cases we don't know exactly why Y works just that it does) - so maybe that's some of it? /2

posts.text: Thank you! The Shedler article helps me pinpoint what I struggle with: it's a framework with a lot of moving parts that are quite disconnected (particularly when you the core theory id/ego/superego). In that, it's quite different from Cognitive Behavioral Therapy which is shaped around a core. /1

record.text: Thank you! The Shedler article helps me pinpoint what I struggle with: it's a framework with a lot of moving parts that are quite disconnected (particularly when you the core theory id/ego/superego). posts.text: For those in the know, how's this? In addition to getting the facts right, does it capture the spirit of the psychodynamic approach?

images.alt:
The theory behind psychodynamic therapy holds that our conscious thoughts reflect only a tiny fraction of what drives our behavior; most of it is driven by our unconscious mind. Our mind, in turn, is comprised of the tripartite id, which instinctively seeks pleasure and immediate gratification; the superego, our moral center that internalizes societal standards; and the ego, which mediates between what the id wants and what the superego permits. When the id and superego are in conflict, our ego resorts to unconscious defense mechanisms like denial, projection, or repression to safeguard us. However, if the ego is overwhelmed by the tension between the id and superego, it can result in psychological issues and maladaptive behaviors. 
The therapeutic solution involves making the repressed conflict between the id and superego conscious for resolution. This often requires revealing the moral standards, rules, and expectations that the superego internalized in childhood, which have become ingrained beliefs about right and wrong, causing anxiety, guilt, and shame.

posts.text:
Give me dynamical systems, attractor basins, multi-level causality, latent variables, how machines learn, subjective awareness, agency ... I'm good.

However, my ego struggles with Ego.

record.text:
Give me dynamical systems, attractor basins, multi-level causality, latent variables, how machines learn, subjective awareness, agency ... I'm good.

However, my ego struggles with Ego.

posts.text:
I enjoy the challenge of taking complex concepts and explaining them in ways that do them justice but nearly everyone can understand.

One that trips me up: psychodynamic theory. It's hard to capture Ego, ID, Superego.

Have you ever seen it done succinctly and well?

archive.org/details/long...

images.alt: circle with ego, superego and id parsed by conscious and unconscious
